title: Mince pies
subtitle: Short pastry cases filled with spiced fruit mincemeat.
excerpt: Buttery shortcrust pastry cases filled with shop-bought or homemade fruit mincemeat and baked until golden. Dusted with icing sugar. Makes twelve. The essential Christmas bake.
sourceNotes: Mince pies in their current form (small pastry cases filled with sweet spiced dried fruit) evolved from the medieval period when meat, fruit, and spices were combined in pastry as a preservation and seasoning technique. The meat gradually disappeared from the recipe over the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, leaving the fruit and spice filling that became the Victorian standard. The association with Christmas is firm by the seventeenth century, when Samuel Pepys records eating them.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled mince pies for up to three months. Reheat from frozen at 180 °C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the pastry and mincemeat quantities for 24 mince pies.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baked mince pies keep in an airtight tin for five days.

content.text: Good mince pie pastry is rich, short, and slightly crumbly rather than tough. The fat is kept cold, worked in quickly, and the pastry is chilled before rolling to prevent shrinkage. Overmixed pastry shrinks in the tin and loses its texture.

Use a 7 cm cutter for the base and a 6 cm cutter for the lid. The base should be a little larger so it can be pressed up the sides of the muffin hole. Do not overfill: a heaped teaspoon of mincemeat per pie is enough.
A 12-hole muffin tin, round cutters (7 cm and 6 cm or similar), and a rolling pin.

Rub {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into {{plain-flour}} of flour and {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar with your fingertips until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add a pinch of salt. Add most of the beaten egg and bring together into a smooth dough with a light hand. Do not overwork it. Flatten to a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180 °C fan. Roll the pastry on a lightly floured surface to about 3 mm thick. Cut out 12 large rounds (about 7 cm) for the bases and 12 smaller rounds (about 6 cm) for the lids. Press the large rounds into the holes of a buttered muffin tin. Add a heaped teaspoon of {{mincemeat}} of mincemeat to each. Top with the small rounds and press the edges to seal. Brush the lids with the remaining beaten egg.
Bake for 16–18 minutes until golden. Leave to cool in the tin for 5 minutes before lifting out — the pastry is fragile while hot. Dust generously with icing sugar before serving.

items.symptom: Pastry has shrunk away from the tin
items.cause: Pastry overworked or not chilled before baking.
items.fix: Chill the pastry for at least 30 minutes before rolling. Do not stretch it when pressing into the tin; let it settle into place.

items.symptom: Mincemeat is bubbling out over the sides
items.cause: Too much filling, or the lid was not sealed properly.
items.fix: Use a heaped teaspoon per pie only. Press the lid edges firmly onto the base.

items.symptom: Pastry is pale and soft after the recommended time
items.cause: Oven temperature varies, or muffin tin holes are deep and the base is not getting enough heat.
items.fix: Add 3–5 minutes and check again. A darker tin conducts heat better for pastry.

items.symptom: Pies are breaking when removed from the tin
items.fix: Leave to cool in the tin for at least 5 minutes. The icing sugar dusting can wait until they are completely cool.

content.text: Mince pies are one of the oldest continuously made baked goods in Britain. The medieval versions were large oval pies containing minced meat and dried fruit together. As the centuries passed, meat left the filling and sugar increased; by the nineteenth century the format was small, individual, and entirely sweet. The debate about whether shop-bought or homemade mince pies are better is one of the reliable annual arguments in British food culture, and the answer depends almost entirely on the quality of the pastry.
